interactions.summary: Sneako presses the danger of further Iran escalation; Jiang says a U.S. retreat could enable a mutually beneficial deal, but instead war creates adrenaline, madness, and a need to win at all costs. He reaffirms that this is the end of American empire.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Sneako asks or frames the issue; Jiang answers in the main analytic voice where present.

speaker_notes.note: Machine diarization is consistent in this packet: SPEAKER_00 is Sneako/interviewer and SPEAKER_01 is Jiang, except where intro/outro material is host-only.

claims.claim: Jiang says Iran would likely negotiate if the United States stopped the war, closed Middle East bases, and offered a mutually beneficial deal around navigation, investment, and dollar-system participation.
He reaffirms that the Iran war marks the end of American empire because war makes actors crazy and drives them toward escalation once they are inside the fight.
He compares war madness to World War I commanders sending millions into machine-gun fire, arguing that irrational escalation becomes normal inside war.

signature_moments.moment: The “art of the deal” exists but is rejected for escalation.
signature_moments.source_phrase: it would bring peace to the world the art of the deal
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the interview’s clearest contrast between a possible deal and the actual war path.

signature_moments.moment: War becomes bodily intoxication: once the fight starts, adrenaline takes over.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It sets up why the later ground-war and draft predictions follow from momentum.
